title: Language Models are Realistic Tabular Data Generators (GReaT)
authors: Vadim Borisov, Kathrin Seßler, Tobias Leemann, Martin Pawelczyk, Gjergji Kasneci (University of Tübingen; Technical University of Munich)
tokenization_method: TEXT SERIALIZATION (subject-predicate-object) + off-the-shelf LLM SUBWORD TOKENIZER, used for GENERATION (the row is the OUTPUT, not just the input). There is no learned per-feature tabular tokenizer. Each cell becomes a clause t_{i,j} = [f_j, 'is', v_{i,j}, ','] joining the COLUMN NAME f_j and the raw VALUE v_{i,j} (Eq.1); a full row is the concatenation t_i = [t_{i,1},...,t_{i,m}] (Eq.2), e.g. 'Age is 39, Education is Bachelors, Occupation is Adm-clerical, Gender is Male, Income is <=50K,'. CRITICAL SECOND STEP — RANDOM FEATURE-ORDER PERMUTATION: at train time the per-feature clauses are shuffled by a random permutation k drawn fresh per row (Def.2), removing the artificial pseudo-positional ordering that text imposes on order-free tabular features. The permuted sentences are then tokenized by the LLM's own BPE tokenizer (GPT-2 byte-pair) and the model is fine-tuned with the standard causal-LM objective p(t)=prod_k p(w_k | w_1..w_{k-1}) (Eq.3). GENERATION: precondition the LLM with (a) just a feature NAME -> samples whole joint p(V_1..V_n); (b) a single name-value pair -> p(V_{\i} | V_i=v_i); (c) MULTIPLE name-value pairs -> p(V_{rest} | V_{i1}=v_{i1},...) = 'arbitrary conditioning'. Permutation training is what makes any feature subset a valid sentence prefix, so arbitrary conditioning costs NO retraining. Next token sampled by temperature-T weighted choice (Eq.4); generated text is regex-parsed back into a row, invalid samples (out-of-support categories, e.g. 'Adm clerical' without dash) discarded (<1% rate, lowered by reducing T).
feature_types_handled: numerical, categorical, and missing — all flattened into one natural-language sentence; no type is declared to the model. Categorical values written verbatim as their string; numerical values written as raw digit strings. Missing values handled implicitly via serialization (a 'Missing' token appears in the sampled output examples, Fig.3). Target variable is treated as just another feature (Remark 1), which is what enables using GReaT for classification/regression data augmentation and imputation. TEMPORAL: not handled — GReaT is explicitly for NON-SEQUENTIAL tabular data; the related-work section notes prior BERT-based time-series work (Padhi 2021) but GReaT itself has no temporal mechanism.
token_granularity: subword BPE tokens of a natural-language sentence (GPT-2 byte-pair). One tabular value has no fixed token count — a number like 32561 or a multi-word category splits into several subwords, and column names span multiple tokens. Sequence length grows with the number of features times their textual verbosity, bounded by the LLM context window. This is the OPPOSITE of FT-Transformer's one-token-per-feature; granularity is whatever the text tokenizer emits.
numerical_strategy: RAW DIGIT-STRING text inside the sentence (e.g. 'Age is 39'); no binning, scaling, piecewise-linear, periodic, or quantile embedding. The appendix (A.1 'Processing of numerical values') explicitly DEFENDS this by citing Wallace 2019 / Brown 2020 / Dinh 2022 that transformers can process number-as-text, but concedes 'smarter encodings for numerical values can also be considered a possible path to further improvement' — i.e. raw-digit serialization is the acknowledged weak point and the explicit handoff to the numerical-embeddings route.
categorical_strategy: the category's natural-language STRING is written verbatim into the sentence (e.g. 'Education is Bachelors', 'Occupation is Adm-clerical'). Open-vocabulary: no per-category lookup table, one-hot, hashing, or target encoding. Semantics ride on the LLM's pretrained word embeddings + the included column name. Authors explicitly contrast this with CTGAN's one-hot mode/category encoding, which 'aggravates the curse of dimensionality in the presence of high-cardinality variables' and 'does not profit from contextual information' — GReaT's text encoding is positioned as the high-cardinality-friendly, context-aware alternative.
high_cardinality_handling: no dedicated mechanism, but the text-as-encoding sidesteps the one-hot blow-up: a high-cardinality categorical costs tokens (sequence length) but NOT a wide one-hot dimension or a large embedding table, and semantically-named categories inherit meaning from LLM pretraining. The argued advantage over CTGAN is exactly this avoidance of the high-cardinality curse-of-dimensionality. BUT this is asserted, not benchmarked: no experiment isolates high-cardinality columns, and the cost reappears as longer sequences / slower autoregressive sampling rather than disappearing.
temporal_handling: none. Explicitly scoped to non-sequential tabular data.
scales_to_many_features: WEAK / UNTESTED for the topic's 70+-feature setting, and COSTLY. The largest real dataset is Diabetes with 47 features (8 numerical + 39 categorical); the largest synthetic one is Alarm with 37 categorical features (Table 7). NO dataset in the paper reaches 70 features, so any 'scales to many features' claim is an extrapolation beyond the evidence. Cost scales badly: each feature adds a clause to every sentence, lengthening the autoregressive generation; runtime (Table 6, 2 GPUs, 100 epochs, 1000 samples) — GReaT fine-tuning 9:10h vs CTGAN 1:35h vs TVAE 0:46min; GReaT sampling 17s vs TVAE 0.28s vs CTGAN 4s for only 1000 rows. So 70+ rich features means longer sequences, super-linear training, and serial token-by-token sampling per row — a generation-throughput wall, not a scalable encoder.
downstream_model: an off-the-shelf transformer-DECODER LLM IS the generator (no separate tabular head). Two sizes evaluated: DistilGPT-2 (82M params -> 'Distill-GReaT') and GPT-2 (355M params -> 'GReaT'). The fine-tuned LLM autoregressively generates the row text. Downstream USE of the generated data: synthetic tables train discriminative models (LR / Decision Tree / Random Forest) for the ML-efficiency metric.
pretraining_objective: no new objective; pure transfer from the off-the-shelf GPT-2 / DistilGPT-2 web-text pretraining, then causal-LM fine-tuning on the textually-encoded, feature-order-permuted rows. The ablation (Table 3) confirms the pretraining matters: 'w/o pretraining' (random-init same architecture) degrades the discriminator metric drastically (e.g. Adult discr. 99.14% vs 69.77% with pretraining; HELOC 99.51% vs 68.29%) and lowers MLE — evidence that the LLM's pretrained world knowledge, accessed via column/category names, drives realism.
key_results: Six real-world datasets (Travel 954 rows, Sick, HELOC, Adult Income 32k, Diabetes 101k, California Housing 20k) + 3 synthetic (Alarm, Asia, GMM). Four metrics, 5 seeds. (1) ML EFFICIENCY (Table 1, train discriminator on synthetic, test on real): GReaT or Distill-GReaT is best or second-best on essentially all dataset/model cells, e.g. Adult RF 85.42 (GReaT) vs Original 85.93 vs CTGAN 83.53 vs TVAE 83.48; HELOC LR 71.90 (GReaT) vs Original 71.80 vs CTGAN 57.72 vs CopulaGAN 42.03. (2) DISCRIMINATOR measure (Table 2, lower=more indistinguishable, 50% ideal): GReaT averages 69.57% vs Distill-GReaT 78.82% vs CTGAN 89.88% vs TVAE 90.73% vs CopulaGAN 90.37% — i.e. GReaT decreases discriminator accuracy ~16.2% over the best baseline; best single results e.g. Adult 62.84% (GReaT) vs CTGAN 97.23%. (3) DCR (distance to closest record): GReaT's distribution matches the original-test-set-vs-train baseline, so it generates near-but-not-copied samples (no memorization shown). (4) Qualitative joint plots (California Lat/Long; Adult Age/EducationNum): GReaT captures strong inter-feature dependencies that CTGAN/CopulaGAN miss.
baselines_compared: CTGAN (conditional GAN for tabular, Xu 2019 — the prior SOTA), TVAE (tabular VAE, Xu 2019), CopulaGAN (Gaussian-copula-simplified CTGAN, SDV framework Patki 2016). GReaT applies ZERO preprocessing (names/values used as-is) whereas all baselines require extensive preprocessing (categorical encoding, scaling, missing-value handling). Discriminative evaluators: LR, Decision Tree, Random Forest. NOTE: no GBDT generative baseline and no other LLM-serialization method (e.g. TabLLM/LIFT) compared.
transferable_idea: Serialize each cell as 'COLUMN_NAME is VALUE' and TRAIN UNDER RANDOM FEATURE-ORDER PERMUTATION so the model becomes order-invariant and supports conditioning on ANY feature subset at inference with no retraining. The permutation trick is the single most reusable nugget for the 70+-heterogeneous-feature setting: it is the mechanism that converts a position-sensitive sequence model into an order-free, arbitrary-conditioning joint model over heterogeneous features, and it is architecture-agnostic (could be applied to any token-sequence tabular model, not only GReaT). The column-name-as-semantics idea (shared with TabLLM and CARTE) is the second nugget. The verbatim-text generation mechanism itself does NOT transfer to a 70+-feature ENCODER pipeline — it is a generator with a serial-sampling cost wall — but the permutation-for-arbitrary-conditioning principle and the order-invariance objective do.
limitations: Authors-stated / evident: (1) RUNTIME: fine-tuning is far slower than VAE/GAN baselines (9:10h vs 0:46min for TVAE on 2 GPUs) and autoregressive sampling is serial and slow (17s for only 1000 rows) — a generation-throughput problem at scale. (2) NUMERICAL precision: numbers are raw digit strings with no numeric-aware encoding; authors flag 'smarter encodings for numerical values' as future work. (3) Out-of-support / invalid generations: the LLM can leave a categorical's support (e.g. 'Adm clerical' without dash, or mixing categories), needing regex validation + rejection (<1%) and lower temperature. (4) PRIVACY: ethics statement explicitly warns owners must verify reverse-identification is impossible before sharing generated data — DCR shows non-copying on average but is not a formal privacy guarantee. (5) Non-sequential only; no temporal support. (6) Permutation makes the learning problem harder and SLIGHTLY hurts MLE (Table 3).
pitfalls: 1) FEATURE-COUNT EXTRAPOLATION (topic-critical): NO evaluated dataset exceeds 47 features (Diabetes 8+39); the topic targets 70+. 'Heterogeneous feature types in various sizes' refers to ROW counts (954 to 101k), NOT feature counts. Do not read GReaT as validated at 70+ columns. 2) GReaT is a DATA GENERATOR, not a discriminative tokenizer — its strong numbers are generation quality (ML-efficiency / discriminator / DCR), not classification accuracy of a tokenizer feeding a deep model; importing it as a 'tokenization recipe' for a 70-feature predictor is a category error (the reusable part is the permutation objective, see transferable_idea). 3) The numerical strategy is raw-digit text with NO numeric embedding (authors concede this) — do NOT import as a numerical-tokenization method; pair with the numerical-embeddings route instead. 4) Serial autoregressive sampling cost (17s/1000 rows for 355M GPT-2) makes large-scale or many-feature generation expensive vs one-shot VAE/GAN decoders. 5) Out-of-support category generation is a real failure mode masked by the <1% rejection rate; the rate would likely grow with more high-cardinality categoricals. 6) No comparison to other LLM-serialization methods (TabLLM/LIFT) and no GBDT generative baseline; SOTA claim is only vs CTGAN/TVAE/CopulaGAN. 7) DCR is a weak privacy proxy, not a formal guarantee — the ethics statement itself cautions against over-reading it.
relevance_to_topic: Canonical GENERATION-side member of the llm-tabular-serialization route: shows a heterogeneous mixed-type row can be modeled with zero learned tabular tokenizer by spelling it as 'name is value' text and fine-tuning GPT-2, AND contributes the feature-order-permutation trick that yields order-invariant, arbitrary-conditioning joint modeling — the most transferable idea for unifying many heterogeneous features into one order-free token sequence. But its evidence tops out at ~47 features and its serial-sampling cost and raw-digit numerics make the literal mechanism a cautionary anchor, not a recipe, for the 70+-feature target.
code_available: yes — https://github.com/kathrinse/be_great ; pip install be-great. Maintained Python package, 3-line API to fit and sample; aligns with the paper (GPT-2 / DistilGPT-2 backbones, textual encoding + permutation + sampling).
